A pump curve usually shows head decreasing as flow rate increases.
A system curve often increases roughly with Q² when friction dominates.
The operating point is the intersection of pump and system curves.
Adding pipe friction always increases the pump efficiency to 100%.
Major losses are associated with pipe length and wall friction.
Minor losses can come from valves, bends, entrances, and exits.
Both major and minor losses can be expressed as head loss.
Minor losses are always numerically smaller than major losses.

A pole on the imaginary axis can produce sustained oscillation in ideal models.
A right-half-plane pole makes the natural response grow.
Zeros alone determine internal stability regardless of poles.
Increasing gain can reduce steady-state error in many loops.
Increasing gain can reduce stability margins.
Very high gain can amplify measurement noise effects.
Proportional gain changes the plant's physical mass directly.
